No laptops, that's new this year. No laptops. No cell phones turned off. Format we're gonna be using. How you should prepare. Full participation, yada, yada, yada, yada, yada. Okay, now, if all you do is give them the rules at the beginning of the year, the whole thing falls apart by the second meeting, so they gotta be reminded and reminded and reminded and reminded. But there's some language you can see about meeting rules of conduct. I was gonna show you here. Page 171 you really don't need to see. 173 you really don't need to see. Let's look at 171 for a second just to show you. So here's the memo that goes to them giving them their phone slots. So here's what your dates are. Here's the rules. It gives me the right to change the days. It reminds them each member as a time slot locked in for the entire year. It tells them not to give the phone number out to anybody else because it's only used for this. It tells them to start a file and put the memo in it. Now you wouldn't think you'd need to tell them to start a file and put the memo in it, would you? I mean, surely everybody knows, keep the memos. No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, they don't know that. And so if you don't tell them, you haven't even got a prayer of them keeping the memos. Go to the memo, throw it out, then they can't find the phone number. Then they can't find anything. Then they don't know what their day is, their time slots. And then there's a decision of how much you choose to baby them and how much you don't. Vicky's been nice about it for a while. I didn't baby him at all. Then Vicky sends them reminders, which I probably wouldn't bother, but it's a decision to be made. Here's how we handle consulting days. This could be instructive for some. So in my program, Platinum One this year, everybody gets a day, so it's part of the deal. Private client group was the same last year. So here's a very recent memo, releasing 12 days in the second quarter, and offering them to this list of people. And now they can respond with their preferences. And then we go back to them and tell them, you got this day, you got this day, you got this day, you got this day. Here's your schedule. And so one fact goes to everybody, back comes their preferences. Vicky puts them on a list. This guy says he can't do this day, he'll take any of these days. This guy says he can't do this day, he'll take any of these days. And then I sit there and decide and put them all in order. And we send another group fax out to everybody that tells everybody when their day is and where they're supposed to be. And then we'll release some more dates to another group of them, a month or so down the road. Now I do the same thing with clients who are on a waiting list by the way. So I also used to control phone this way when I traveled. So there'd be 50 people who've piled up waiting, they wanna talk to me on the phone. And instead of sending phone appointments, what I used to do is I used to run, you know, the radio calling deal. We used to send them all fax with all their names listed on it. And Dan's gonna be sitting in the Marriott in Tupelo for three hours before he goes over to speak at the Peter Law that. So from one to three, he's taking calls. Here's a list of everybody who's been waiting to talk to him. Have at it, okay? But don't leave messages, because he's not gonna check any messages, he's not gonna call anybody back. Whoever gets through, gets through. Whoever don't get through, doesn't get through. And next time he's gonna be sitting somewhere, we'll send you another fax. That's the way we used to handle clients and prospective clients. And I'd sit there with a list, check them off, you know. And some of the course would leave messages and then a week later be, how come he didn't? Cause it says right there, he wouldn't go and call you back. You either got through or you didn't get through. Now as goofy as that sounds, he was pretty efficient actually. And I mean, prospective clients, fortune 1,000 companies, doesn't make any difference. I've been dialing for two hours, getting a busy. Well, I finally got you. Well, hurry up, cause we've only got 10 minutes left. I gotta walk out of the door here. You know, what do you wanna talk about? And that's the way I used to handle phones when I traveled. Now that I'm stationary, and there's less of them, I do it by appointments. So these are all like wording and documents and things that you might use to exercise control over folks.
